speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're walking through one of the most common open source contribution patterns: you make your own copy of a project, work in that copy, and then ask the original project to accept your changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is the workflow people use when they want to help with a project but they are not one of the core maintainers?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. This is the fork-based workflow, and it's foundational. It's also the workflow used for the capstone when a repository accepts pull requests. We'll go from the big idea all the way through forking, cloning, branching, committing, pushing, opening a pull request, responding to review, syncing your fork later, and cleaning up after a merge.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And we should say this up front: there are related appendices if someone needs more depth. Advanced Git, branch protection, and Git authentication can all become relevant while doing this.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. And if you ever need the official current source of truth, GitHub Docs, the GitHub changelog, the docs pages on GitHub flow, pull requests, issues, and contributing to a project are the authoritative references.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's start with the core concept. A fork is your personal copy of someone else's repository on GitHub. When you fork a repository, GitHub creates a full copy under your account. You can push to it, create branches on it, and modify it freely without changing the original repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the original repo stays safe, and my copy is where I experiment and prepare my contribution.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's right. The original repository is usually called the upstream repository. Your fork is linked conceptually to that upstream repo, which is what makes it possible to open a pull request from your fork back to the original project.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why do forks exist in the first place?

speaker: ALEX
text: They solve a permissions problem. Most open source projects do not give every contributor write access to the main repository. Instead, you fork the project, make changes on your copy, and open a pull request asking the maintainers to merge your changes. That allows broad participation without maintainers handing out direct write access.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it's a collaboration model and a security model.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And there's a useful contrast with earlier work where you had write access to a shared learning repository. In that case, you could branch directly on the repository itself. In open source, you usually don't have that permission, so you fork first and then create branches on your fork.

speaker: JAMIE
text: People often mix up fork, clone, and branch. Can we separate those clearly?

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely. A fork lives on GitHub under your account. It creates a copy of an entire repository on GitHub. A clone lives on your computer. It creates a local copy of a repository so you can work with files and Git commands. A branch lives inside a repository, local or remote. It's a named pointer to a specific commit history, and it lets you isolate a change.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I'm contributing to a project I don't own, the sequence is: fork on GitHub, clone to my computer, create a branch inside that local clone, do the work, push that branch back up to my fork, and then open a pull request to upstream.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfect. And for learners who benefit from a mental picture, think of three locations. On the left is the upstream repository on GitHub. In the middle is your fork on GitHub. On the right is your local repository on your computer. The fork action goes from upstream to your GitHub account. The clone action goes from your fork to your computer. Editing, staging, and committing happen locally. Pushing sends your branch from your computer to your fork. And the pull request asks upstream to accept the changes from your fork.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That description is especially helpful for audio learners and screen reader users because it translates the diagram into a spatial model.

speaker: ALEX
text: And there are some practical accessibility checks worth remembering. If you run git remote dash v in the terminal, you can hear which remotes are configured. Usually origin is your fork and upstream is the original repository. In VS Code, the status bar shows the current branch name. You can navigate there with F6 and confirm you're on a feature branch instead of main. And if you use the Command Palette with Control Shift P, then choose Git: Checkout to, a screen reader will announce branch names in the picker.

speaker: JAMIE
text: For low vision users, the fork URL includes your username, which makes it visually different from the upstream URL. And in VS Code, the bottom-left branch indicator and the Source Control panel heading help confirm both branch and repository.

speaker: ALEX
text: And on GitHub.com, sighted users can also notice the "forked from" label near the repository name.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's do the workflow. For this lesson, the example repository is Community-Access slash accessibility-agents.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And first comes the fork itself.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. If you're using the browser on GitHub.com, go to the upstream repository page. Find the Fork button near the upper-right area of the page. Screen reader users can cycle through buttons with the B key in NVDA or JAWS. The Fork button appears near the top, after Watch and Star. VoiceOver users can open the buttons rotor with VO+U and navigate to Fork.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then GitHub opens the create-a-new-fork page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Usually the defaults are correct. The owner should be your username. The repository name stays accessibility-agents. There's also a checkbox to copy the main branch only, and for this workflow you leave that checked. Then activate Create fork.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And after that, GitHub redirects me to my own copy, something like github dot com slash my username slash accessibility-agents.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. At that point, you have your own copy and the upstream repo is untouched.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if someone prefers GitHub CLI?

speaker: ALEX
text: They can create the fork from the command line with: `gh repo fork Community-Access/accessibility-agents --clone=false` That creates the fork on GitHub without cloning yet.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There was also a handy tool card version of this workflow, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. In a browser, you fork first, then use the green Code button to copy a clone URL. In VS Code Desktop, you still need the browser for the actual fork, then use Git: Clone in the Command Palette. In GitHub Desktop, you fork in the browser first, then use File, Clone Repository and choose your fork. And GitHub CLI can do both fork and clone in one command with `gh repo fork Community-Access/accessibility-agents --clone` followed by changing into the directory.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, now I have a fork on GitHub, but no local files yet.

speaker: ALEX
text: That means it's time to clone your fork locally. If you're using VS Code, open the Command Palette with Control Shift P or Command Shift P on macOS, type Git: Clone, press Enter, paste your fork URL, choose a local folder such as Documents, and then accept the prompt to open the cloned repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I'm in the terminal, it's the classic two commands: `git clone https://github.com/your-username/accessibility-agents.git` and then `cd accessibility-agents`.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. In GitHub Desktop, go to File, then Clone Repository, choose the GitHub.com tab, find your fork, choose a local path, and click Clone. And with GitHub CLI, use `gh repo clone your-username/accessibility-agents` and then change into the repository directory.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should I know immediately after cloning?

speaker: ALEX
text: After cloning, your local repository typically has one remote called origin, and origin points to your fork.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's an important distinction. Origin is not automatically the original project. It's usually wherever I cloned from.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And in this fork workflow, that means origin usually points to your fork.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now comes the part many beginners miss: adding upstream.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. This matters because you want your local repository to know about both destinations: your fork and the original project. Origin points to your fork. Upstream should point to the original repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why is that so useful?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because the upstream project keeps moving. New commits land there all the time. If you want to stay current, update your local main branch, or start fresh feature branches from the latest code, you need a remote that points to the original repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So how do I add it?

speaker: ALEX
text: From the terminal, run: `git remote add upstream https://github.com/Community-Access/accessibility-agents.git` That defines a second remote named upstream.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then I verify with `git remote -v`.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You should hear or see two remotes. Origin should reference your username slash accessibility-agents. Upstream should reference Community-Access slash accessibility-agents. Verification matters because mixing them up can cause real confusion.

speaker: ALEX
text: Once remotes are set up, you create a feature branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Not on main.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. Not on main. Create and switch to a new branch before you make changes. In the terminal, that typically looks like `git switch -c your-branch-name` or, on older setups, `git checkout -b your-branch-name`.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What kind of branch names are helpful?

speaker: ALEX
text: Short, descriptive names. Something like `fix-readme-typo`, `add-agent-example`, or `update-install-steps`. The branch name should hint at the change.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about in VS Code?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the branch indicator in the status bar or the Command Palette. You can invoke a branch creation or checkout command and create a new branch there. In GitHub Desktop, there's a branch menu for creating a new branch and switching to it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then we verify again.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. In the terminal, `git branch --show-current` tells you the current branch. In VS Code, the status bar branch name confirms it. In GitHub Desktop, the current branch control does the same. This is one of those habits that prevents accidental commits to main.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now we're finally ready to make changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and this part is not just technical. There are contribution principles too. Keep your changes focused. Try not to mix unrelated edits into one pull request. Read any contributing guidelines the project provides. And if the repository has issue templates or specific conventions, follow them.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So good contributions are easier to review because they are scoped and understandable.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Make the smallest meaningful change that solves the problem you're addressing. Then stage and commit your changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you walk through that plainly?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. First, edit the files. Then check what changed with `git status`. Stage what you want to include with `git add` and commit with `git commit -m "clear message here"`.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the commit message should say what changed, not just "stuff" or "update."

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Something concise and specific. For example, "Add clarification to setup instructions" or "Fix broken link in README."

speaker: JAMIE
text: How does this feel in VS Code?

speaker: ALEX
text: In VS Code, the Source Control panel shows changed files. You can review them, stage them there, type a commit message into the message box, and commit. For screen reader users, that panel can be navigated structurally, and the file list gives a clear inventory of what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And one reassuring point from the source: multiple commits are fine.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Beginners sometimes think a pull request must be one perfect commit. It does not. Multiple commits are completely acceptable, especially while you're iterating or responding to review feedback.

speaker: ALEX
text: After you commit locally, you need to push your branch to your fork.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is where the branch leaves my computer and appears on GitHub.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The first push of a new branch usually needs an upstream tracking relationship. In the terminal, that often means: `git push -u origin your-branch-name` The `-u` sets the default upstream for that branch so later pushes can often just be `git push`.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And subsequent pushes become simpler because Git remembers where that branch goes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. After the first push, additional commits on that same branch can usually be sent with `git push`.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about graphical tools?

speaker: ALEX
text: In VS Code, after you commit, you can use the sync or publish branch prompts. In GitHub Desktop, you'll see a Push origin button. The exact wording can vary a bit, but the idea is the same: send the branch to your fork.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do I verify that worked?

speaker: ALEX
text: Go to your fork on GitHub.com and confirm the branch exists there. Often GitHub shows a banner suggesting you open a pull request for the recently pushed branch. Even without the banner, the branch dropdown should show your new branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now for the public-facing part: opening the pull request.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A pull request, or PR, asks the upstream project to review and potentially merge your changes. One common path is the GitHub.com banner that appears after you push a branch. You activate Compare and pull request.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if the banner isn't there?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can go to the Pull Requests tab on GitHub.com and choose to create a new pull request manually. Then verify the direction carefully.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's the subtle part, isn't it? Making sure the base repository is upstream and the compare branch is from my fork.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The base should be the upstream repository, usually on its main branch. The compare side should be your fork and your feature branch. This is one of the most important things to double-check in a fork workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if someone prefers the command line?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub CLI supports this too with `gh pr create`. It walks you through repository and branch choices, and often opens prompts for title and body.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What makes a good PR description?

speaker: ALEX
text: Explain what changed, why it changed, and anything a reviewer should pay attention to. If the change addresses an issue, mention that issue. Keep it clear and respectful. The goal is to help maintainers review efficiently.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And this is another place where accessibility matters. A well-structured PR with a meaningful title and body is easier for everyone, including screen reader users who may navigate by headings, links, and landmarks.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's say the maintainers review the PR and request changes. What happens then?

speaker: ALEX
text: That's normal. You stay on the same branch, make the requested edits locally, commit them, and push again to that same branch. The pull request updates automatically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So I do not usually create a brand-new PR just because feedback came in.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. Same branch, same PR, more commits.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And what does good review etiquette look like?

speaker: ALEX
text: Be courteous. Assume good intent. Respond to comments directly. If you disagree or you're confused, ask respectful questions. Reviewers absolutely expect questions from new contributors. If requested changes are unclear, replying with a clarifying question is better than guessing silently.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's encouraging, because many learners hesitate to reveal uncertainty.

speaker: ALEX
text: And open source collaboration works better when uncertainty is visible and discussed.

speaker: ALEX
text: There's another maintenance skill that matters a lot over time: keeping your fork in sync.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Because upstream keeps changing, and my fork can become stale.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. If your fork drifts too far behind, you may see conflicts or start new work from outdated code. The command-line sync process is very systematic.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Walk us through it step by step.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, make sure you are on main. `git switch main` Then download the latest changes from upstream. `git fetch upstream` Then merge upstream changes into your local main. `git merge upstream/main` And finally push the updated main branch to your fork. `git push origin main`

speaker: JAMIE
text: So that sequence updates local main from upstream, then updates my fork's main to match.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And those four ideas matter conceptually: be on main, fetch upstream, merge upstream into local main, push local main back to origin.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can this also be done without the command line?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. On GitHub.com, fork pages often include a Sync fork control that can bring your fork up to date with upstream. GitHub CLI also has sync capabilities for forks.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When should I sync?

speaker: ALEX
text: Before starting a new branch, definitely. Also if your pull request falls behind, or any time you haven't contributed in a while and want a fresh foundation. Starting from an up-to-date main branch reduces friction.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there were learning-card style reminders here too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The big takeaway is to treat syncing as a normal maintenance habit, not as a rescue move only when something breaks.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Could we do a compact checklist of the whole workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Fork the upstream repository to your GitHub account. Clone your fork locally. Add the upstream remote. Create a feature branch. Make your changes. Stage and commit them. Push the branch to your fork. Open a pull request to upstream. Respond to review feedback on the same branch. After merge, sync your fork again when needed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then cleanup.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, cleaning up after merge is part of a tidy workflow. Delete the local branch when you're done, typically after switching away from it first. For example, switch to main, then delete the branch with a command like `git branch -d your-branch-name` if it's fully merged.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the remote branch on my fork can be deleted too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. You can delete the remote branch on GitHub, often from the merged pull request page or with Git commands. The point is to reduce clutter once the work has landed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we finish, can we cover the "what if something goes wrong" section? Because this workflow has many places where a newcomer can get stuck.

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely. If push is rejected because your branch is behind, fetch the latest changes, merge main into your branch if needed, resolve any conflicts, and push again. If a reviewer requested changes and you do not understand them, reply to the review comment with a question. That is normal and expected.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about "repository not found" when cloning?

speaker: ALEX
text: Double-check the URL and make sure the fork actually exists under your account. A tiny typo in the username or repository name is enough to cause that error.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone accidentally committed to main instead of a feature branch?

speaker: ALEX
text: That's recoverable. The advanced Git appendix covers ways to move commits onto a new branch. The important thing is not to panic.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There was also guidance for learners who finish and still aren't sure whether they did it right.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Compare your work against the relevant reference solution if one is provided. It does not need to match exactly. The goal is a sound workflow and a meaningful contribution, not identical wording.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone wants the cultural context behind this workflow, that ties back to earlier material on open source culture. If they want the next practical destination, the next lesson continues into accessibility agents.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. So here's the big picture to carry with you: a fork lets you contribute safely to projects you don't control, a clone gives you a place to work locally, a branch keeps your change isolated, and a pull request opens the door to collaboration. Once you understand that chain, open source becomes much more approachable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And much less mysterious.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the goal.
